content: The efficient frontier represents the set of portfolios offering maximum expected return for each level of risk. Portfolios below the frontier are suboptimal because a higher return can be achieved at the same risk level.

Mean-variance optimization solves min(w^T * Sigma * w) subject to w^T * mu = target_return and sum(w) = 1. The quadratic program produces optimal weights but is highly sensitive to estimation errors in mu and Sigma.

The Black-Litterman model combines market equilibrium returns (derived from market cap weights via reverse optimization) with investor views using Bayesian updating. The formula is: E[r] = [(tau*Sigma)^(-1) + P^T*Omega^(-1)*P]^(-1) * [(tau*Sigma)^(-1)*Pi + P^T*Omega^(-1)*Q].

Risk parity allocates portfolio weights so each asset contributes equally to total portfolio risk. For asset i, the risk contribution is RC_i = w_i * (Sigma * w)_i / sqrt(w^T * Sigma * w), and the goal is RC_i = RC_j for all i, j.

Hierarchical Risk Parity (HRP) uses dendrogram clustering on the correlation matrix to build a tree structure, then allocates inversely proportional to cluster variance using recursive bisection. HRP avoids matrix inversion and is more robust than mean-variance optimization.

The Fama-French 5-factor model explains stock returns as: R_i - R_f = alpha + beta_MKT*(R_m-R_f) + beta_SMB*SMB + beta_HML*HML + beta_RMW*RMW + beta_CMA*CMA + epsilon. The five factors are market risk, size (small minus big), value (high minus low book-to-market), profitability (robust minus weak), and investment (conservative minus aggressive).

The maximum diversification portfolio maximizes the diversification ratio DR = (w^T * sigma) / sqrt(w^T * Sigma * w), where sigma is the vector of individual asset volatilities. This ratio equals 1 only when all assets are perfectly correlated.

The minimum variance portfolio minimizes total portfolio variance min(w^T * Sigma * w) without any return constraint. It has a closed-form solution: w* = Sigma^(-1) * 1 / (1^T * Sigma^(-1) * 1), where 1 is a vector of ones.

The Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage estimator improves covariance estimation by shrinking the sample covariance matrix S toward a structured target F: Sigma_shrunk = delta*F + (1-delta)*S. The optimal shrinkage intensity delta is determined analytically to minimize expected loss.

Robust portfolio optimization addresses estimation error by using uncertainty sets around expected returns. Instead of point estimates, it solves max_w min_mu {w^T*mu : mu in U} where U is an ellipsoidal uncertainty set, leading to a second-order cone program.

Transaction costs in portfolio optimization include explicit costs (commissions, exchange fees, taxes) and implicit costs (bid-ask spread, market impact). A realistic model adds a penalty term: min w^T*Sigma*w + lambda*sum(c_i*|w_i - w_i_current|) to the objective.

Rebalancing frequency involves a tradeoff: frequent rebalancing keeps the portfolio closer to optimal weights but incurs higher transaction costs. Calendar rebalancing (monthly/quarterly) is simple but suboptimal; threshold rebalancing (trigger at 5% drift) is more cost-effective.

The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) states E[R_i] = R_f + beta_i*(E[R_m]-R_f), where beta_i = Cov(R_i, R_m)/Var(R_m). While foundational, CAPM is rejected empirically because size, value, and momentum effects are not captured by market beta alone.

The Sharpe ratio measures risk-adjusted return as SR = (R_p - R_f) / sigma_p. A Sharpe ratio above 1.0 is considered good, above 2.0 is excellent. The Sortino ratio improves on this by using only downside deviation in the denominator.

The Kelly criterion determines optimal position sizing: f* = (p*b - q) / b, where p is win probability, q = 1-p, and b is the win/loss ratio. Full Kelly is aggressive; practitioners typically use half-Kelly (f*/2) to reduce drawdown risk by ~50%.

Maximum drawdown measures the largest peak-to-trough decline in portfolio value. The Calmar ratio (annualized return / max drawdown) and Sterling ratio are drawdown-based risk metrics. A max drawdown exceeding 20% often triggers behavioral selling in retail investors.

Tail risk measures include Value-at-Risk (VaR) at confidence level alpha: the loss not exceeded with probability 1-alpha, and Conditional VaR (CVaR/Expected Shortfall) which averages losses beyond VaR. CVaR is coherent while VaR is not subadditive.

Factor investing constructs portfolios with systematic exposure to return-generating factors. Common equity factors include market, size, value, momentum, quality, and low volatility. Factor returns have time-varying premia and can experience prolonged drawdowns.

The information ratio IR = alpha / tracking_error measures active return per unit of active risk relative to a benchmark. A portfolio manager with IR > 0.5 is considered good; IR > 1.0 is exceptional and rare over sustained periods.

Mean-CVaR optimization replaces variance with Conditional Value-at-Risk in the objective: min CVaR_alpha(w) subject to w^T*mu >= target. This can be reformulated as a linear program using auxiliary variables, making it computationally tractable for large portfolios.

Portfolio turnover is defined as sum(|w_new - w_old|)/2 and represents the fraction of the portfolio traded. High turnover erodes returns through costs; a good target is under 20% monthly for active strategies.

Sector and geographic constraints in portfolio optimization add linear inequality constraints: sum(w_i for i in sector) <= max_sector_weight. Typical institutional limits are 30% per sector, 40% per country, and 10% per individual security.

The covariance matrix for N assets has N*(N+1)/2 unique parameters. With 500 assets, that is 125,250 parameters estimated from limited time series. This dimensionality curse makes shrinkage, factor models, or random matrix theory essential for stable estimates.

The tangency portfolio lies at the point where the capital market line (from the risk-free rate) is tangent to the efficient frontier. Its weights are w* = Sigma^(-1)*(mu - R_f*1) / (1^T*Sigma^(-1)*(mu - R_f*1)), and it maximizes the Sharpe ratio.

Out-of-sample performance of optimized portfolios is typically 30-50% worse than in-sample. Techniques to improve robustness include resampled efficient frontier (Michaud), bootstrap aggregation of weights, and Bayesian estimation with informative priors.
